agents-access.title: Agents as first-class principals
agents-access.summary: Register an agent, get its automatic keypairs, and exchange its API key for a short-lived JWT.

blocks.text: In 1Claw an agent is a real principal with its own identity, not a shared human login. An agent can authenticate three ways: an API key with the ocv_ prefix, mutual TLS, or OIDC federation. This lesson uses the API key path because it's the fastest to run end to end.

items: API key: ocv_ random value, argon2-hashed, shown once at creation
mTLS: client certificate identity for infrastructure agents
OIDC: federate an external identity provider into 1Claw

blocks.text: When you register an agent, 1Claw auto-generates an Ed25519 signing keypair and a P-256 ECDH keypair. Private keys live HSM-encrypted in the __agent-keys vault; only the public keys sit on the agent record.

steps.text: Get a human user API key (prefix 1ck_) from the dashboard at Settings then API Keys. Agent keys cannot create other agents, so you need a user key here.
Register an agent. The plaintext ocv_ key is returned once in this response and never again. Set token_ttl_seconds and vault_ids to scope the token.
Save the agent_id and api_key from the response, then exchange them for a JWT. This endpoint needs no bearer token; the credentials in the body are the auth.
Confirm the token works by fetching the agent's own profile. You should see the ssh_public_key and ecdh_public_key fields.

blocks.text: You now have a registered agent and a fresh JWT bound to its vaults and TTL. Every later call in this track uses that Bearer token.

quiz.question: How many times is the plaintext ocv_ API key returned?
options: Once, at agent creation or key rotation
Never, it stays in the HSM
quiz.explanation: Only the argon2 hash is stored; the plaintext is shown once at creation or rotation.

quiz.question: Which endpoint turns an agent_id plus api_key into a JWT?
quiz.explanation: POST /v1/auth/agent-token takes the credentials in the body and returns a short-lived access_token.

quiz.question: What two keypairs does 1Claw auto-generate for a new agent?
quiz.explanation: Every agent gets an Ed25519 signing keypair and a P-256 ECDH keypair at creation.

agents-access.title: Policies and scoped access
agents-access.summary: Grant an agent read access to exactly one secret path with a glob policy.

blocks.text: A fresh agent has zero access. Its JWT carries an empty scope list until a human writes a policy. Policies tie a principal to path patterns and permissions, so you decide exactly what an agent can touch.

items: * matches one path segment
** matches zero or more segments (any depth)
read: GET a value and list metadata
write: PUT and DELETE at matching paths

blocks.text: When you create, update, or delete an agent's policy, all of that agent's active JWTs are revoked immediately. The agent must re-exchange its API key to pick up the new scope.

steps.text: Start with a human user key and the vault and agent UUIDs from the previous lesson.
Create a policy that lets the agent read only api-keys/openai. The pattern is a single path, so the agent cannot reach anything else in the vault.
Re-exchange the agent key for a fresh JWT. The old token was revoked by the policy change.
Read the allowed path (works), then try a different path (returns 403).

blocks.text: Policies also support runtime conditions: an IP allowlist (specific IPs or CIDR ranges) and a time window (allowed hours and days). Add these when an agent runs from known infrastructure.
Your agent can now read one secret path and nothing else, and any policy change takes effect on the very next request.

quiz.question: What access does an agent have before any policy is written?
options: Read on its own vault only
Read plus write on the default vault
quiz.explanation: 1Claw is safe-by-default: no scopes and no policies means the JWT grants nothing.

quiz.question: In a policy pattern, what does ** match?
options: Zero or more segments at any depth
quiz.explanation: * matches one segment while ** matches zero or more segments (any depth).

quiz.question: What happens to active JWTs when an agent's policy changes?
options: They gain the new scope automatically
quiz.explanation: Any policy create, update, or delete revokes the agent's active JWTs so stale scopes cannot linger.

agents-access.title: Agent self-enrollment and approvals
agents-access.summary: Let an agent enroll itself with no credentials and route the approval to a human.

blocks.text: Sometimes the agent boots before anyone has provisioned it. The enroll endpoint is public, so an agent can request its own identity and wait for a human to approve. The API key is emailed to the human after approval and never returned to the agent.

steps.text: No credentials are required. Submit an enrollment with a name and an optional human_email. With an email, Allow and Deny links are sent to that address.
steps.code:
curl -s -X POST https://api.1claw.xyz/v1/agents/enroll \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"deploy-bot","human_email":"alice@example.com","description":"CI pipeline agent"}'

steps.text: If you send name only, the response includes an approval_url the human opens while signed in. No email needed to start.
The human approves via the emailed link or the approval_url. After approval an agent is created in their org and the ocv_ key is emailed to them.
The human then writes a policy (see the policies lesson) so the new agent can read the paths it needs.

blocks.text: For sensitive actions an agent can also file an approval with POST /v1/approvals/request, directed to its creator. Requests carry a risk tier: T1 is informational, T2 requires biometric step-up, and T3 requires passkey plus TOTP attestation before the human can approve.
Enrollment is rate limited: one per email per 10 minutes when email is used, plus caps on pending rows and per-IP limits. Some branches return a uniform 201 to limit email enumeration.
You can now bootstrap an agent from zero credentials and hand the approval to a human, who receives the key and grants scoped access.

quiz.question: Where does the enrolled agent's API key go?
options: Emailed to the human after approval
quiz.explanation: The plaintext key is emailed to the approving human and is never returned from the enroll endpoint.

quiz.question: Which risk tier requires passkey plus TOTP attestation?
quiz.explanation: T1 is informational, T2 requires biometric, and T3 requires passkey plus TOTP.

quiz.question: What can an agent submit with name only and no email?
options: A link-only enrollment returning an approval_url
A full agent with an immediate key
quiz.explanation: Name-only enrollment creates a link-only pending enrollment and returns an approval_url for a signed-in human.

agents-access.title: MCP server integration
agents-access.summary: Connect an AI client to the 1Claw MCP server and run a first list_secrets call.

blocks.text: The 1Claw MCP server gives an AI client just-in-time access to vault secrets over the Model Context Protocol. The hosted server runs at https://mcp.1claw.xyz/mcp and authenticates per connection with a Bearer JWT and an X-Vault-ID header. The MCP server itself enforces nothing: every call is checked against the agent's policies by the vault API.

steps.text: Mint an agent JWT with your agent_id and ocv_ key. This is the Bearer value the client sends.
Add the hosted server to your client config, for example Claude Desktop at claude_desktop_config.json. Include the vault UUID in X-Vault-ID.
Prefer local stdio? Run the server as a process and pass only the API key. It exchanges the key for a JWT, auto-refreshes, and auto-discovers the vault.
Restart the client and ask it to list your secrets. The agent calls list_secrets and returns paths and metadata, never values.
steps.code: List the secrets in my 1claw vault.

blocks.text: Output inspection runs by default. If a known secret value ever appears in a non-secret tool's output, it is replaced with [REDACTED:#hashprefix] before it reaches the model context.
Your AI client is now wired to the vault, and a first list_secrets call returns metadata scoped to the agent's policies.

quiz.question: Which two headers authenticate a hosted MCP connection?
quiz.explanation: The hosted server takes a Bearer JWT in Authorization and the vault UUID in X-Vault-ID.

quiz.question: Who enforces access control for MCP tool calls?
quiz.explanation: The MCP server just relays requests; the vault API checks every call against the agent's policies.

quiz.question: What does list_secrets return?
options: Paths, types, versions, and metadata only
quiz.explanation: list_secrets returns metadata and never secret values; use get_secret for a value.

advanced-security.title: Key hierarchy and the HSM
advanced-security.summary: See how envelope encryption keeps a stored secret useless without KMS access.

blocks.text: 1Claw encrypts every secret with envelope encryption. A random 256-bit DEK encrypts the value with AES-256-GCM, then a per-vault KEK that never leaves the HSM wraps the DEK. In production the HSM is Google Cloud KMS at FIPS 140-2 Level 3 protection.

items: JWT signing key: one Ed25519 key per deployment, in the same KMS key ring
KEK: one symmetric key per vault, wraps and unwraps DEKs
DEK: fresh 256-bit key per secret version, never persisted in the clear
KEKs rotate on a 365-day period; KMS keeps old versions for decrypt

blocks.text: Every KMS encrypt, decrypt, and sign includes a CRC32C checksum. If the checksum mismatches in transit, the operation fails immediately instead of returning corrupt data.

steps.text: Mint an agent JWT (or use a user key) with write access to a vault.
Store a secret. On the wire this is plaintext; at rest 1Claw generates a DEK, encrypts the value, and wraps the DEK with the vault KEK.
Read the metadata back. Reason about the row: the database holds only ciphertext, the wrapped DEK, IV, and tag. Without a KMS unwrap none of it decrypts.

blocks.text: You stored a secret and can now explain why a stolen database is worthless: the plaintext DEK exists only in memory during the operation, and the KEK never leaves the HSM.

quiz.question: What wraps the DEK before it is stored?
options: The per-vault KEK inside the HSM
quiz.explanation: Each vault has one KEK in KMS that wraps and unwraps that vault's DEKs.

quiz.question: What FIPS level is the production HSM?
quiz.explanation: Production KMS keys use HSM protection at FIPS 140-2 Level 3.

quiz.question: How often do KEKs rotate automatically?
quiz.explanation: KEKs rotate on a 365-day period, the NIST SP 800-57 maximum for symmetric KEKs.

advanced-security.title: Customer-managed keys (CMEK)
advanced-security.summary: Add a client-side AES-256-GCM layer so 1Claw stores only your key's fingerprint.

blocks.text: CMEK double-encrypts secrets. You encrypt the value with your own AES-256-GCM key in the browser or your process, then 1Claw applies its HSM envelope encryption on top. Your key never touches the server; only its SHA-256 fingerprint is stored so the vault knows which key was used. CMEK is available on Business and Enterprise plans.
The client-side wire format is one version byte (0x01), a 12-byte IV, the ciphertext, then a 16-byte GCM auth tag. A full server compromise yields only doubly-encrypted blobs.

steps.text: Install the SDK and have a user token plus a vault UUID ready.
Generate a 256-bit key and its fingerprint. Store the key safely because you need it for every read and write.
Enable CMEK on the vault by sending only the fingerprint.
From now on encrypt before writing and decrypt after reading. The cmek_encrypted flag on a GET response tells you the value needs client-side decryption.

blocks.text: To rotate, send the old and new keys to POST /v1/vaults/:id/cmek-rotate. The server re-encrypts in batches of 100, atomically per batch, and holds both keys in memory only for the duration of the job.
The vault now requires your key for every value, and 1Claw cannot decrypt your secrets unilaterally.

quiz.question: What does 1Claw store about your CMEK key?
options: An encrypted copy in the HSM
quiz.explanation: Only the SHA-256 fingerprint is stored so the vault can track which key was used.

quiz.question: What algorithm does the client-side CMEK layer use?
quiz.explanation: CMEK encrypts values client-side with AES-256-GCM before the HSM layer is applied.

quiz.question: What protects a secret if the CMEK vault's database is stolen?
options: It is doubly encrypted and useless without your key
Only the fingerprint is needed to decrypt
quiz.explanation: Each secret carries two independent encryption layers, so a backup yields only doubly-encrypted blobs.

advanced-security.title: MPC secret storage
advanced-security.summary: Split a secret's DEK across parties so no single holder can reconstruct it.

blocks.text: MPC splits each secret's DEK into shares so one compromised holder cannot decrypt. It layers on top of the standard HSM envelope encryption and is set per vault. The custody mode is immutable once set.

items: 2of2_client_custody: XOR split between the server HSM and you; you hold a client_share
2of3_multi_hsm: Shamir 2-of-3 across GCP, AWS, and Azure; no client share
2of3_client_custody: Shamir 2-of-3 with two HSMs plus your client share

blocks.text: For client-custody modes, store the client_share safely. Lose it on a 2of2_client_custody vault and the secret cannot be recovered, because the server holds only its single share.

steps.text: Use a user key on a Pro or higher plan and a vault you own.
Enable MPC with XOR client custody. This choice is permanent for the vault.
Write a secret. The PUT response includes a base64 client_share you must keep.
Read it back by sending your share in X-Client-Share. Without the header the read fails.

blocks.text: The vault now returns a client_share on write and requires it on read, so 1Claw alone cannot reconstruct the DEK.

quiz.question: Which mode returns a client_share and needs X-Client-Share on reads?
quiz.explanation: The 2of2_client_custody XOR split gives you a client_share and requires it on every read.

quiz.question: Can a vault's MPC custody mode be changed after it is set?
quiz.explanation: The custody mode is immutable once set on a vault.

quiz.question: Where are the shares stored in 2of3_multi_hsm?
quiz.explanation: 2of3_multi_hsm distributes three Shamir shares across GCP KMS, AWS KMS, and Azure Key Vault.

advanced-security.title: Shroud TEE proxy
advanced-security.summary: Route a chat completion through Shroud so it is inspected inside a hardware TEE.

blocks.text: Shroud is an OpenAI-compatible LLM proxy running inside a confidential GKE node with AMD SEV-SNP. Your agent sends chat completions to Shroud instead of the provider. Shroud authenticates the agent, resolves the provider key, runs threat detection and secret redaction, then forwards the request. Secrets never leave your infrastructure in the clear.

items: Endpoint: POST https://shroud.1claw.xyz/v1/chat/completions
X-Shroud-Agent-Key: agent_id:api_key (the agent's ocv_ key)
X-Shroud-Provider: openai, anthropic, google, gemini, and others
Secret redaction, PII detection, and prompt-injection scoring run before the model sees the prompt

blocks.text: Prompt injection is scored 0.0 to 1.0. Anything above 0.9 is a hard block regardless of the agent's configured threshold, which defaults to 0.7.

steps.text: Use a Shroud-enabled agent. Store the provider key in the vault at providers/openai/api-key and grant the agent read access, or pass X-Shroud-Api-Key directly.
Send a chat completion. Shroud resolves the provider key from the vault when X-Shroud-Api-Key is omitted.
Read the response. It follows the standard OpenAI shape, so the content is at choices[0].message.content.

blocks.text: No editor supports the X-Shroud headers natively. Run npx @1claw/cli proxy locally to bridge Cursor, Claude Code, and Copilot traffic into Shroud.
Your agent's LLM traffic now flows through the TEE proxy, inspected and redacted, with a normal OpenAI-style response coming back.

quiz.question: What is the format of the X-Shroud-Agent-Key header value?
quiz.explanation: The value is exactly agent_id:api_key, with the agent's ocv_ key on the right.

quiz.question: At what prompt-injection score does Shroud hard-block regardless of config?
quiz.explanation: Scores above 0.9 are always blocked, even if the agent's threshold is higher.

quiz.question: What TEE technology runs the Shroud proxy?
quiz.explanation: Shroud runs on GKE confidential nodes using AMD SEV-SNP.

advanced-security.title: Zero trust and the risk engine
advanced-security.summary: Plant a honeytoken canary so any read trips a critical verdict and revokes sessions.

blocks.text: 1Claw assumes no implicit trust. Every request is authenticated, authorized against policy, and audited to a tamper-evident SHA-256 hash chain. Revocation is immediate: delete a policy and access stops on the next request. On top of that, the risk engine scores every authentication event in real time.

items: Geo-velocity: impossible travel between two locations
First-seen: a new ASN, country, or device for this principal
Honeytokens: decoy secrets whose access records a critical verdict
DPoP binds a JWT to a client keypair (RFC 9449)

blocks.text: A critical verdict triggers Continuous Access Evaluation: all of that principal's active tokens are revoked, their JTIs are added to the revoked list, and their next request fails auth. Verdicts carry a 15-minute TTL.

steps.text: Use a user token and a vault UUID. Pick a path that looks valuable so an attacker is tempted.
Create a honeytoken. Reading this decoy later marks the reader for denial.
steps.code:
curl -s -X POST https://api.1claw.xyz/v1/risk/honeytokens \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"vault_id":"<vault-uuid>","secret_path":"api-keys/production","notes":"Canary for vault breach"}'

steps.text: Confirm it is armed by listing your honeytokens.

blocks.text: When a honeytoken is read, the current request is not blocked so the attacker is not tipped off. Their next authentication attempt is denied instead.
You now have a live canary: any read of api-keys/production records a critical verdict and revokes that principal's sessions.

quiz.question: What does the audit log use to stay tamper-evident?
quiz.explanation: Audit entries form a SHA-256 hash chain, and inserts go through a SECURITY DEFINER function.

quiz.question: What happens the moment a honeytoken is read?
options: A critical verdict is recorded but the request proceeds
Nothing until a human reviews it
quiz.explanation: The read is allowed to avoid tipping off the attacker, but a critical verdict blocks their next auth.

quiz.question: What does DPoP protect against?
options: Use of a stolen JWT without the matching private key
quiz.explanation: DPoP binds a JWT to a client keypair, so a stolen token is useless without the private key.
